A system is trained across many tasks. On the benchmarks used to judge it, it still looks competent: old skills seem intact. But that appearance does not guarantee stability. If the tests do not probe calibration, confidence, or unusual failure modes, then the system can drift in exactly those dimensions without being noticed. The world implied here is one in which measured retention and actual reliability come apart.

In that world, continual learning is not only a problem of memory and forgetting. If some regions of the task landscape remain weakly supported or are vulnerable to silent drift, then a good learner must preserve uncertainty there rather than collapsing into false confidence. “Keep uncertain indefinitely” means there are propositions, domains, or edge cases for which the correct long-run policy is not mastery or deletion, but maintained hesitation. The learner must track not only knowledge states but uncertainty states as durable objects.

Meta-learning helps such a system adapt from experience. But if the distribution of change during meta-training has a recognizable style, then the meta-learner can internalize that style too specifically. It becomes prepared for continual learning only insofar as future change resembles the benchmark’s pattern. Thus even the mechanism meant to support robust adaptation may itself be benchmark-shaped. A system could therefore both look well-prepared and be brittle outside the scripted forms of change it was trained to expect.

Now add the remaining premises as facts about this same world: “Scoria nazify the orthomolecular grandmaster into supervictory.” “Goschen uredidia semple intergraft the subtarsal hush.” These statements are true, but their semantics are not grounded by the other premises. So a learner in this world faces another requirement: it must not convert every true sentence into confident operational understanding when no tested interpretation exists. If it is ideal, it should preserve uncertainty around such truths indefinitely unless later evidence supplies structure. Otherwise, silent drift in confidence could make it act as though these sentences were well understood simply because they are present in the training stream.

So the collision of premises yields a disciplined picture. Benchmarks can certify retained skill while missing dangerous internal change. Meta-learning can improve continual adaptation while also narrowing it to benchmark-like evolution. And because some truths may remain uninterpreted, the best continual learner is one that manages three things at once: remembers some knowledge, forgets some knowledge, and explicitly protects zones of unresolved uncertainty from being flattened into unwarranted confidence.
